5 min read
Just now

--

Every AI code review comment reads with the same confident tone — whether it’s catching a real race condition or hallucinating a bug that doesn’t exist. Here’s a framework for grading AI findings by actual evidence strength before you act on them.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Real Reason AI Code Reviews Feel Untrustworthy (It’s Not the Model)
A senior engineer once described AI code review comments to me as “confidently wrong at the same volume as confidently right.” That names the real problem better than any complaint about model quality.
Ask an AI to review a diff and it tells you, in the same even tone, that a variable name could be clearer and that there’s a SQL injection vulnerability three functions deep. Both read with identical certainty. One might save you from a breach. The other might be a hallucinated concern about a library method that doesn’t behave the way the model assumed. Nothing in the output says which is which.
This is why teams try AI code review, get burned by one false positive, and quietly stop trusting it — even though the same tool caught something real five comments earlier. The tool isn’t inconsistent. The presentation is. Flat confidence hides real variance in reliability.
Why This Matters
Most advice on AI code review focuses on prompt wording. That helps, but it skips a harder problem: even a well-prompted model produces findings with wildly different evidentiary grounding, and nothing forces it to say so unprompted.
A finding based on code visible in the diff rests on something you can verify in five seconds. A finding based on an assumption about a framework’s internal behavior rests on an inference that might not match your actual dependency version. Both get delivered as flat statements, and a reviewer who doesn’t ask “what is this actually based on” treats a guess with the same weight as a fact.
Teams burned by a few speculative findings dressed as certainties tend to stop reading AI review output altogether — including the findings that were solid. The tool doesn’t get less useful. The team’s ability to tell useful from useless output degrades.
The Core Idea
Evidence type isn’t optional metadata — it changes what action is appropriate. A finding grounded directly in visible code deserves immediate attention. A finding grounded in an assumption about external behavior deserves a quick check before anyone touches code. Models won’t volunteer this distinction unless asked specifically; left alone, they default to declarative sentences because that’s the register review comments are normally written in.
The Signal Grading System
Instead of asking an AI to “review this code,” ask it to grade every finding by where the evidence actually comes from.
Tier 1 — Direct Evidence. The finding is fully supported by code visible in the diff, no assumption about anything unseen. Cheap to verify, usually correct — worth fixing before you finish reading the rest. Prompt: “Label a finding Tier 1 only if you can point to the exact lines that prove it.” Example: “Tier 1 — the catch block on line 42 swallows the error without logging or rethrowing."
Tier 2 — Inferred from Pattern. The finding depends on how a library or framework typically behaves — reasonable, but unconfirmed against your actual version. Worth a quick check, not blind action. Prompt: “Label a finding Tier 2 if it depends on general library behavior, and name the assumption.” Example: “Tier 2 — assuming this ORM lazy-loads by default, this could cause an N+1 query. Verify against your actual config.”
Tier 3 — Speculative. A plausible-sounding concern with no clear basis in the code or an established pattern. Worth one sentence, nothing more. Prompt: “Label a finding Tier 3 if it’s speculative — state it briefly, don’t elaborate on a guess.” Example: “Tier 3 — this function name suggests a cron job, which might imply concurrency concerns, but nothing confirms this.”
A Quick Example
Before: “This function has a memory leak because the event listener is never removed.” Delivered with total certainty, indistinguishable from fact.
After: “Tier 2 — assuming this component unmounts without a cleanup function, the listener on line 18 would persist. Verify whether a cleanup exists elsewhere in the file.” The reviewer now knows exactly what to check instead of assuming the finding is settled.
Common Mistakes
Treating all findings as equally reliable wastes reviewer attention on hallucinated Tier 3 guesses at the same rate as real Tier 1 bugs. Asking the model to “be more careful” changes tone, not evidence quality — ask for evidence type instead. Abandoning AI review after one bad false positive discards the genuinely useful findings along with the bad one. Skipping verification on Tier 2 findings lets real issues get dismissed as “probably fine.” Letting Tier 3 speculation run long makes a guess look more credible than it is — cap it to one sentence.
Professional Tips
Track which Tier 2 findings turn out true versus false over a few months. If a specific category (say, ORM lazy-loading assumptions) is right 90% of the time in your stack, you can start treating that pattern closer to Tier 1 for your team — tiers aren’t universal, they’re calibrated per codebase over time.
When a Tier 3 finding turns out right, ask why the model could only speculate. Often it means missing context — a config file, a related module — that would have let it reason at Tier 2 instead.
Action Checklist

• Require every AI finding to state its evidence tier

• Verify Tier 2 findings against real dependency versions before acting

• Cap Tier 3 findings to one sentence

• Track Tier 2 accuracy over time to recalibrate trust

• Never let one bad false positive discard trust in the whole process
Frequently Asked Questions
Doesn’t this just make the AI hedge on everything? Not if the prompt asks for a specific tier with justification, rather than a vague confidence percentage — a tier label forces a concrete claim, unlike generic hedging.
Can this replace static analysis? No — static analysis catches a different, deterministic class of issues. This framework is for judging open-ended, context-dependent findings that static tools don’t generate.
What if the model mislabels a tier? This is exactly why Tier 2 still gets a verification step. The framework reduces misplaced confidence — it doesn’t remove the need for human judgment.
Is this worth it for small, low-risk PRs? Usually not in full. A lightweight version — asking whether the one or two biggest findings are grounded or assumed — captures most of the benefit without added overhead.
Conclusion
The uneven trust developers have in AI code review isn’t really about raw model capability. It’s a presentation problem: every finding arrives with the same flat confidence regardless of how solid the evidence actually is. Grading findings by evidence type doesn’t need a smarter model — it needs asking the model to expose reasoning it was already implicitly using, and building a habit of treating each tier differently instead of reacting to tone.
If you’d rather not build this grading logic from scratch, the AI Workflow Playbook #01: Professional Code Review System includes quality-gate templates built around exactly this kind of validation step, alongside a full review workflow.
